front: Poem: Samuel Johnson adapted its 10th section as "The Vanity of Human Wishes"
Author: coined the phrase "bread and circuses" (panem et circenses) to mock the Roman public's distraction
Author: coined the phrase "quis custodiet ipsos custodes?" / "who watches the watchmen?"
Work: the phrase "who watches the watchmen?" from it provided the title of a graphic novel by Alan Moore
Author: coined "a sound mind in a sound body" (mens sana in corpore sano) in his 10th Satire
Work: consists of 16 poems in dactylic hexameter criticizing Roman society and mores
Poem: Juvenal's tenth satire mocks this Carthaginian general's deluded ambitions
Poem: programmatic opener declaring that "it is difficult not to write" satire
Poem: complains about women participating in boar hunts and the exiled Marius Priscus spending all afternoon drinking
Poem: speaking through his friend Umbricius, the poet declares that Rome is no place where "no decent Roman" belongs
Poem: Samuel Johnson adapted it as "London," which begins "Tho' Grief and Fondness in my Breast Rebel" and has Thales leaving the title city
Poem: in Dryden's "Paraphrase" of it, a "penniless exile" becomes a poet resembling Dryden himself
Poem: Dryden inserted the line "When the King's Trump, the Mob are for the King" into his version of it
Poem: a Council is called to deal with how to cook a huge turbot that is too large for any pan
Poem: the courtier Crispinus buys a huge fish for 6,000 sesterces, baffling Domitian's counselors, and Montanus suggests potters craft a giant vessel
Poem: describes the worst possible dinner party, in which the patron Virro humiliates a poor client
Poem: the host sips old wine "bottled when Consuls wore their hair long" and orders distilled water "cooler than frost in Thrace"
Poem: the host is served a Messina lamprey while the addressee gets "the stringy snake's relative" from "a denizen of that drain beneath the heart of Subura"
Poem: concludes that "you won't be afraid to endure the whip: that's the dinner and friend you're worthy of"
Poem: a diatribe against marriage addressed to Postumus, urging him to hang himself from the Aemilian bridge or take a boy lover rather than marry
Poem: source of "quis custodiet ipsos custodes?", which in context mocks the decline of womanly virtue rather than government institutions
Poem: claims that "luxury" is "more damaging than any foe" and notes that "we are now suffering the calamities of a long peace"
Play: Truewit's anti-feminist anti-marriage diatribe in it is adapted from Juvenal's Satire 6
Poem: describes a cringing husband closing his eyes after his drunk wife vomits "like a snake" on a marble floor
Poem: Samuel Johnson's adaptation of it begins "Let observation with extensive view / Survey mankind, from China to Peru"
Poem: advises one to pray for a sound mind in a sound body and a brave heart with no fear of death
Poem: Juvenal's Twelfth Satire is on this topic
Poem: titled "On education in avarice," its opening bemoans those who bring safety deposit boxes rather than wallets to the tables and a barber who becomes richer than the wealthy
Poem: refers to the Old Testament as "the sacred volume of Moses" and blames a father's observance of the Sabbath as the "cause and culprit" of his child's vices
Poem: describes stories of Hercules and the Minotaur as frivolous, depicting instead a society where husbands steal money from their wives' friends
Poem: claims men are crueler than animals, citing as evidence a riot in Egypt in which a man was torn to pieces and eaten
Poem: Juvenal's fragmentary final satire, whose surviving portion discusses the advantages soldiers have over ordinary citizens
Author: full name Decimus Junius Juvenalis
Author: fragments of the Augustan poet Sulpicia survive as two lines quoted by a scholiast on this Roman satirist
